Making sense of
everyday life
through data.

I study how older adults move through their daily routines, using wearable sensors, semi-supervised learning, and activity recognition to build technology that genuinely supports aging and care.

Explore my research Get in touch
About me

Researcher, analyst,
and lifelong learner

I am Sayeda Shamma Alia, a 4th-year PhD candidate in Electrical and Computer Engineering at Concordia University, Montreal. My research sits at the intersection of machine learning, activity recognition, and assistive technology for elderly populations.

Before my PhD, I completed my MEng in Human Intelligence Systems at Kyushu Institute of Technology in Japan, where I was part of SozoLab — a lab pioneering sensor-based activity recognition. I organized and participated in five international activity recognition challenges, building deep experience leading diverse international teams and working with real-world sensor data.

I have lived and worked across Bangladesh, Japan, and Canada — each country has shaped how I think about people, patterns, and the quiet rhythms of daily life that my research tries to understand.

Leadership & collaboration

Organizing international research challenges

Between 2019 and 2021, I organized and co-organized five international activity recognition challenges at premier venues including UbiComp. These challenges brought together researchers from across the world — requiring me to coordinate international teams, manage datasets, communicate across cultures and time zones, and deliver results under pressure. Each challenge produced a peer-reviewed publication.

2021 · UbiComp

Research

Understanding daily life
from the inside out

As populations age, understanding daily routines becomes critical for early detection of cognitive and physical decline. My PhD work addresses this through three intertwined research directions:

01
📡

Sensor-based activity recognition

Classifying human activities from accelerometer, wearable, and smart-home sensor data — especially in messy, real-life conditions far from controlled lab settings.

Core foundation
02
🤖

Semi-supervised learning

Developing methods that work effectively even when labeled data is scarce — a critical practical challenge in healthcare, where annotation is costly and time-consuming.

PhD focus
03
🔁

Routine detection

Identifying recurring behavioral patterns in sequences of daily activities for elderly adults — enabling early warning signals for cognitive and functional decline.

Application

Why it matters: This research enables smarter assistive technologies, caregiver support systems, and early-warning tools for age-related decline — directly contributing to the growing field of ambient assisted living (AAL).

Projects & experience

Research in practice

Office workers' behavior prediction

Analyzed data from Fitbit, Omron accelerometers, and questionnaires to predict employee behavioral patterns across multiple companies.

RWearablesMulti-modal

Weather prediction at scale

Handled big historical weather datasets on Google AI Platform; led interpolation and Kriging spatial analysis for regional forecasting.

PythonGoogle CloudBig Data

Nurse activity recognition (SICU)

Led a team collecting and analyzing multi-sensor data from nurses in an intensive care unit to classify care activities and support shift planning.

PythonTeam leadHealthcare

MCoMat: Performance metric for imbalanced datasets

Designed a novel evaluation metric for multi-layer activity recognition datasets with class imbalance — a common and critical challenge in real-world sensor data.

PythonEvaluation metricsImbalanced data
Beyond the lab

Noh theatre performer

While living in Japan, I studied Noh — one of the world's oldest theatrical traditions, dating back to the 14th century. I trained rigorously and performed on stage in front of an audience of 500 people.

Learning Noh as a Bangladeshi researcher in Japan required the same qualities I bring to my work: patience, deep attention to detail, and genuine curiosity about cultures far from my own.

🎭 Performed live · 500-person audience · Kitakyushu, Japan

Contact

Let us connect

I am open to research collaborations, internship opportunities, postdoctoral discussions, and industry positions related to human activity recognition, assistive technology, or applied machine learning.

Whether you are a researcher, a healthcare tech company, or a team working on ambient assisted living — I would love to hear from you.
